title: Braiding a rag rug
subtitle: Plaiting fabric strips into a coiled flat rug using recycled cloth
excerpt: A braided rag rug is made from long strips of fabric plaited into a three-strand braid and coiled flat, with each row laced to the previous one. The technique requires no loom and very little equipment: strips are cut from old clothing, sheets, or curtain fabric, and the only tool needed to coil and join the braid is a blunt needle and lacing thread. A small oval or round rug can be completed in an afternoon.
sourceNotes: Braided rag rugs are a documented American and northern European folk textile technique. Method is observation-led.

content.text: folded to hide raw edges, then plaited into a three-strand braid. The braid is coiled flat, and

joins each row to the previous coil. Medium-weight woven fabric works best: denim, cotton twill, and wool flannel are all suitable. Knitted or stretchy fabrics are more difficult to keep flat. Mix colours and textures freely: the visual effect of a braided rug depends on the combination across rows.

items.qty: approximately 500 g per 30 x 40 cm rug
items.name: Woven fabric strips, 4 to 5 cm wide (old clothing, sheets, curtains)
Lacing thread (strong cotton or linen cord)

content.text: Step 1: Cut and prepare the strips

Cut fabric into strips 4 to 5 cm wide and as long as possible. Join strips end to end by cutting a small notch in each strip end and threading one through the other to form a flat join. Fold the long edges of each strip inward to the centre and fold once more to produce a folded strip with no raw edges showing.

Secure three strips together at one end (a safety pin through all three, clipped to the back of a chair, gives hands-free tension). Plait the three strips in a standard three-strand braid, keeping the folds facing outward. Maintain even tension: a tight braid produces a stiffer, more durable rug; a loose braid is softer but less durable.

For an oval rug: lay the first 15 to 20 cm of braid straight, then curve it back on itself to begin the coil. Thread the lacing cord on the needle and stitch through the loops of both the outer and inner rows, pulling them together without bunching. For a round rug: coil the braid from the very first loop. Work the lacing as the braid grows, not after.

To finish: taper the end of the braid by trimming each strand progressively shorter over the last 15 cm. Fold the tapered end under and stitch it flat to the last row with lacing thread. A clean taper makes the join invisible. Press the finished rug flat under a damp cloth and a weight overnight.

attrs.intro: Two common problems with braided rugs.

items.fix: Ease the lacing tension so adjacent rows sit alongside each other without pulling. The lacing should connect but not compress the rows. Re-stitch the tight section before continuing.
items.cause: Lacing is too tight, pulling adjacent rows together too firmly.
items.symptom: Rug cups upward and will not lie flat

items.fix: Use the notch-and-loop join throughout. Knots create lumps inside the braid and can work loose under the tension of coiling and lacing.
items.cause: Strip joins were knotted rather than using the notch-and-loop method.

attrs.heading: Common problems
